summary: My full name is Howard Gentry Barnett Junior, born on June 10th, 1950, in Kansas City, Missouri. I was adopted at birth and flew to Tulsa when I was just weeks old, where I have lived ever since. I grew up in Tulsa, attended Central High School, then Tulane University before transferring to the University of Tulsa, graduating in 1972. Later, I went to Southern Methodist University for law school, graduating in 1975, and practiced law in Tulsa at a small firm that eventually became Sneedling, Adams, and Barnett. My family background and education shaped who I am, and I have always valued the advice my mother gave me about pursuing a diversified education.

I was born in Kansas City, Missouri, on June 10th, 1950. My parents flew there to adopt me, and I had my first plane ride when I was only a few weeks old, flying back to Tulsa. Although I was born there, I have lived in Tulsa ever since and have no particular interest in my biological parents or my birth city beyond that early connection.

Tulsa has been my home since I was a few weeks old after my adoption. My mother’s family moved here in 1919 when my grandfather bought the Tulsa Tribune, and I grew up attending Central High School here. My father started the Brookside State Bank in Tulsa in 1949, and I practiced law here for many years. Tulsa is deeply tied to my family history and my own life story.

My mother, Florence Lloyd Jones Barnett, was born in Madison, Wisconsin, and came from a family with deep Wisconsin roots. She moved to Tulsa in 1919 when her father bought the Tulsa Tribune and graduated from Central High School in 1930. She was well-educated, having attended the University of Wisconsin, and was a very interesting, well-read woman who volunteered extensively but did not work outside the home. She gave me the best advice I ever received about pursuing a diversified education, which shaped my college choices and personality.

Jenkin Lloyd Jones was my uncle and a significant figure in our family. He was the first Unitarian minister west of the Appalachian Mountains and famously rode on the Hope ship with Henry Ford. He was well known in his time and part of the broader Lloyd Jones family legacy that includes many notable individuals.

Richard Lloyd Jones was my grandfather and the owner, editor, and publisher of the Tulsa Tribune after buying it in 1919. He was a first cousin of Frank Lloyd Wright, and he built and lived in Westhope, a Frank Lloyd Wright-designed home in Tulsa. He ran the newspaper until his death and was a central figure in my family’s history.

The Tulsa Tribune was owned and published by my grandfather, Richard Lloyd Jones, who bought it in 1919 and renamed it from the Tulsa Democrat. It stayed in our family until it closed in 1992. Although it was a significant family enterprise, it was not a frequent topic of conversation at home.

Madison, Wisconsin, is where my mother, Florence Lloyd Jones Barnett, was born and raised. The Lloyd Jones family has deep roots there, with many members attending the University of Wisconsin. Madison represents the origin of much of my maternal family’s history before they moved to Tulsa.

The University of Wisconsin–Madison was the alma mater of many in my family, including my mother, her brothers, and my grandparents. It was a central institution for the Lloyd Jones family, reflecting our Wisconsin roots. My mother graduated from there before moving back to Tulsa.

My mother graduated from Central High School in Tulsa in 1930 after the family moved here in 1919. I also attended school in Tulsa, though my own high school years coincided with a turbulent time in the nation during the late 1960s. Central High was part of my family’s Tulsa story.

I was admitted early to Rice University with plans to study engineering because I liked math and science. However, after meeting a girl who was going to Sophie Newcomb in New Orleans, I applied and got into Tulane instead. Although we later broke up, my mother advised me to attend Tulane for a more diversified education, which I did, and it changed my personality.

I started my college education at Tulane University in New Orleans after my mother encouraged me to pursue a diversified education. I spent a couple of years there before transferring back to the University of Tulsa. My time at Tulane was influential in shaping who I became.

My father, Howard Gentry Barnett Senior, grew up in Gregory, Texas, a small town near Corpus Christi. His parents died young, and he had to leave Southern Methodist University to care for his younger sisters. Gregory was the place where his early life and family responsibilities shaped his character.

My father attended Southern Methodist University but had to leave before graduating to take care of his younger sisters after his parents died. Later, I attended SMU for law school, graduating in 1975. SMU is thus connected to both my father’s and my own educational journeys.

My father founded Brookside State Bank in Tulsa in 1949 and ran it until we sold it in 1985. He was a man of great integrity and honesty, though not outgoing, and the bank was a significant part of his professional life. The bank’s history is intertwined with our family’s story in Tulsa.

Frank Lloyd Wright was a first cousin of my grandfather, Richard Lloyd Jones, through family ties in Wisconsin. His architectural legacy includes Westhope, the home my grandfather built and lived in Tulsa, and the Price Tower in Bartlesville. I also visited his winter home and studio at Taliesin West in Scottsdale, Arizona, and knew his last wife, Olgivanna.

Westhope is the Frank Lloyd Wright-designed home built by my grandfather, Richard Lloyd Jones, in Tulsa. He lived there until he died, and it stands as a remarkable piece of family and architectural history. Though I don’t know the full origin of the name, it connects back to a place my grandfather knew in Wisconsin.

Bartlesville is where the Price Tower, designed by Frank Lloyd Wright, is located. The tower was commissioned by Harold C. Price Sr., head of the HC Price Company, and is notable as Frank Lloyd Wright’s only skyscraper. It is part of the architectural heritage connected to my family’s story.

Price Tower in Bartlesville, Oklahoma, was designed by Frank Lloyd Wright as the headquarters for the HC Price Company, led by Harold C. Price Sr. It is considered Wright’s only skyscraper and a significant architectural landmark. I am familiar with its history and its connection to the Price family and Wright.

Harold C. Price Sr. was the head of the pipeline construction company HC Price Company and commissioned Frank Lloyd Wright to design the Price Tower in Bartlesville. His role was pivotal in creating this unique architectural landmark. I knew about him through my interest in the Price Tower and its history.

The HC Price Company was a pipeline construction firm led by Harold C. Price Sr. They commissioned Frank Lloyd Wright to design their headquarters, the Price Tower, in Bartlesville. This company played a key role in the development of that architectural project.

I visited Scottsdale, Arizona, several weeks ago with Margaret, where we saw Frank Lloyd Wright’s winter home and his working lab at Taliesin West. It was a fascinating place and gave me a deeper appreciation for Wright’s work and legacy. Scottsdale is important as the location of this architectural and historical site.

Taliesin West in Scottsdale, Arizona, was Frank Lloyd Wright’s winter home and architectural school. I visited there and learned about its role as a training ground for young architects. I also knew Olgivanna Lloyd Wright, Frank’s last wife, who held court there with formal dinners.

Olgivanna Lloyd Wright was Frank Lloyd Wright’s last wife, and I had the opportunity to know her. She was very involved at Taliesin West, where she held formal dinners and maintained the legacy of Wright’s architectural school. She was an important figure in preserving his work after his death in 1959.

The Hope ship was notable because my great-uncle Jenkin Lloyd Jones rode on it with Henry Ford. This event is part of the family lore and highlights the prominence of the Lloyd Jones family in their time. It connects to the broader history of my family’s involvement in significant social and cultural movements.

Henry Ford is connected to my family history through the Hope ship, on which my great-uncle Jenkin Lloyd Jones traveled with him. This association reflects the notable circles my family was involved in during the early 20th century. It is part of the rich legacy of the Lloyd Jones family.

Unitarian Universalism is tied to my family through my great-uncle Jenkin Lloyd Jones, who was the first Unitarian minister west of the Appalachian Mountains. He was a significant religious figure and helped spread Unitarianism in the region. This faith tradition is part of our family’s heritage.

Robert F. Kennedy’s assassination in 1968 was a major event during my senior year of high school, a turbulent time in our nation. It was part of the backdrop to my education and youth, alongside other significant events like Martin Luther King Jr.’s assassination and the chaotic 1968 Democratic National Convention. These events shaped the era I grew up in.

The assassination of Martin Luther King Jr. in 1968 was a defining moment during my high school years. It was one of several major national tragedies that marked that year, including Robert F. Kennedy’s assassination and the 1968 Democratic National Convention. These events influenced the social and political climate I experienced as a young person.

The 1968 Democratic National Convention was a chaotic and memorable event that occurred during my senior year of high school. It was part of a turbulent year marked by the assassinations of Robert F. Kennedy and Martin Luther King Jr. These events formed the backdrop of my late adolescence and early education.

After starting college at Tulane, I transferred to the University of Tulsa and graduated in 1972. The University of Tulsa was where I completed my undergraduate education and solidified my academic foundation before going on to law school. It is an important part of my educational journey.

I practiced law at a small boutique firm in Tulsa that was originally called Sneedling and Trotter when I joined. Over the years, it grew and became Sneedling, Adams, and Barnett by the time I left nearly ten years later. The firm was small but grew during the oil boom of the 1970s and 1980s, and it was where I built my legal career.
